confidence.disclosure: Reconstructed from Jahangir's own Tuzuk-i Jahangiri (Jahangirnama), the day-by-day imperial memoir he maintained from 1605 to 1622, continued thereafter by Mu'tamad Khan. The Tuzuk records virtually every camp and halt on his Agra-Lahore-Kashmir-Ajmer circuits with month and often day precision. This file selects approximately 60 of the most historically or geographically significant stops; the Tuzuk documents hundreds more routine camps on royal progresses that are omitted here.

sources: Wheeler Thackston (trans.), The Jahangirnama: Memoirs of Jahangir (1999)
Ruby Lal, Empress: The Astonishing Reign of Nur Jahan (2018)

journey.event: Born at Fatehpur Sikri on 31 August 1569, the long-awaited son of Akbar, named Salim after the Sufi Shaykh Salim Chishti.
Accompanied Akbar on the long residence at Attock during the Kabul campaigns.
Established a rebel court at Allahabad against his father Akbar for three years.
Enthroned at Agra on 3 November 1605 following Akbar's death; began the Tuzuk on his accession.
Marched to Lahore to crush his son Khusrau's rebellion; had the Sikh Guru Arjan executed here.
Visited Kabul and revelled in the gardens of his grandfather Babur.
Returned to Lahore and held court there.
Returned to Agra as the main imperial residence.
Married Mehr-un-Nissa (later Nur Jahan) at Agra in May 1611.
Set out from Agra to Ajmer to personally supervise the Mewar campaign.
Visited the Hindu pilgrimage lake of Pushkar and noted it in the Tuzuk with approval.
Held court at Ajmer for three years (1613-1616) while Prince Khurram campaigned in Mewar.
Received the English ambassador Sir Thomas Roe at Ajmer in January 1615.
Moved his court from Ajmer to the Malwa hilltop palace-city of Mandu for the Deccan campaign.
Passed through Ujjain on the march to Mandu.
Held court at Mandu for a year during the Deccan campaign.
Moved the court to Ahmedabad in Gujarat; complained about the heat, dust, and plague in the Tuzuk.
Visited the port of Cambay and saw the sea for the first time.
Returned to Agra after two years in Gujarat.
Halted at Mathura on the Yamuna on the progress toward Kashmir.
Passed through Delhi.
Camped at Panipat, visiting the graves of his ancestors.
Noted a Mughal imperial hunting halt at Karnal.
Continued north through Sirhind.
Arrived at Lahore, the staging point for all Kashmir trips.
Crossed the Chenab at Wazirabad on the road to Kashmir.
Stopped at Bhimbar at the foot of the Pir Panjal range before the ascent into Kashmir.
Crossed Rajouri on the Pir Panjal route to Kashmir.
Arrived at Srinagar, his favourite city; recorded the flora, fruit, and saffron fields of Kashmir in loving detail.
Supervised the laying out of the Shalimar Bagh garden on Lake Dal.
Wintered at Srinagar, recording the Kashmir snow in the Tuzuk.
Returned to Lahore from the first extended Kashmir stay.
Returned to Agra; received news of Prince Khurram's (future Shah Jahan's) rebellion.
Moved to Ajmer to coordinate the suppression of Khurram's rebellion.
Continued directing operations from Ajmer.
Moved the court to Lahore after Khurram's defeat.
Mahabat Khan seized the emperor in a coup at the Jhelum river crossing in March 1626.
Travelled under Mahabat Khan's control to Kabul.
Nur Jahan engineered his rescue near Rohtas Fort later in 1626.
Returned to Lahore after the rescue.
Made his final trip to Kashmir in the summer of 1627.
Fell gravely ill on the return journey from Kashmir at Bhimbar in the Pir Panjal foothills.
Died on the road at Rajouri (or nearby Chingus Sarai) on 28 October 1627.
Buried at Shahdara Bagh on the banks of the Ravi at Lahore, where Nur Jahan later built his tomb.
